name: allra-error-handling
description: Allra 백엔드 에러 핸들링 및 예외 처리 표준. Use when handling errors, creating custom exceptions, or implementing error responses.
capabilities: Creates custom exception hierarchies following the BusinessException pattern
Designs structured error response DTOs with error codes and field validation details
Defines ErrorCode enums with HTTP status codes and error messages
Implements proper logging strategies with appropriate log levels
Adds entity-level exception classes for domain-specific error scenarios
author: Allra-Fintech
securitySummary: All static findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation-only skill containing Java code examples for error handling patterns. No actual security risks present.
valueStatement: Java Spring applications often lack consistent error handling patterns. This skill provides a complete error handling standard including exception hierarchies, global exception handlers, and structured error responses.
limitations: Focuses on Java Spring Boot applications only
Does not provide code for error monitoring or alerting integration
Does not include internationalization (i18n) for error messages
Does not generate code for distributed tracing or error correlation

useCases.title: Build Consistent API Error Responses
useCases.description: Design a standardized error response format across all REST endpoints with consistent error codes, messages, and field-level validation details.

useCases.title: Implement Domain-Specific Exceptions
useCases.description: Create a hierarchy of custom exceptions for business logic errors such as EntityNotFoundException, DuplicateResourceException, and authorization failures.

useCases.targetUser: Full-stack developers working on API backends
useCases.title: Add Centralized Exception Handling
useCases.description: Implement a global exception handler that catches and processes all exceptions uniformly, ensuring consistent error responses to API clients.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Error Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a business exception hierarchy for a Java Spring Boot application. Include a base BusinessException class, an ErrorCode enum with common HTTP error codes, and examples of domain-specific exceptions like UserNotFoundException.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting a new Spring Boot project and need error handling foundations

promptTemplates.title: Global Exception Handler
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement a @RestControllerAdvice GlobalExceptionHandler that handles BusinessException, MethodArgumentNotValidException for validation errors, and generic Exception. Return a structured ErrorResponse with timestamp, error code, message, and field errors.
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding centralized error handling to an existing API

promptTemplates.title: Structured Error Responses
promptTemplates.prompt: Design an ErrorResponse record in Java that includes error code, message, a list of field-level errors with field name, rejected value, and error message, plus a timestamp. Provide factory methods for different error scenarios.
promptTemplates.scenario: Creating a consistent API error response format

promptTemplates.title: Service Layer Error Patterns
promptTemplates.prompt: Show how to use custom exceptions in a Spring service layer. Include examples of orElseThrow with EntityNotFoundException, duplicate resource validation with DuplicateResourceException, and authorization checks with ForbiddenException.
promptTemplates.scenario: Writing service methods with proper error handling

bestPractices: Define a centralized ErrorCode enum with consistent HTTP status codes and error codes for all business exceptions
Use WARN level logging for expected business exceptions and ERROR level for unexpected system exceptions
Exclude sensitive data from error responses and log messages to prevent information leakage
antiPatterns: Throwing generic Exception or RuntimeException instead of custom business exceptions
Exposing stack traces or internal error details directly to API clients
Using string concatenation instead of parameterized logging for structured log output

faq.question: How does this skill differ from Spring's default error handling?
faq.answer: This skill provides a standardized, enterprise-grade error handling pattern with custom exception hierarchies, structured error responses, and consistent logging. Spring's default error handling is basic and not suitable for production APIs.

faq.question: Can I use this with non-Spring Java applications?
faq.answer: The exception hierarchy and error response patterns can be adapted to any Java application. However, the @RestControllerAdvice examples are specific to Spring Boot.

faq.question: How do I handle validation errors from request bodies?
faq.answer: Use @Valid on request body parameters and catch MethodArgumentNotValidException in the GlobalExceptionHandler. Extract field errors from the BindingResult and include them in the ErrorResponse.

faq.question: Should I include stack traces in error responses?
faq.answer: No. Stack traces expose internal implementation details and can leak sensitive information. Always return user-friendly messages in production environments.

faq.question: How do I log errors properly without exposing sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use parameterized logging with placeholders like log.warn("User not found: userId={}", id). Never directly log user input, passwords, or sensitive fields.

faq.question: Can I extend this pattern for microservices?
faq.answer: Yes. Add correlation IDs to ErrorResponse for distributed tracing. Consider standardizing ErrorCode enums across services for consistent error handling in microservice architectures.
